title: Barefoot Daily News Bulletin — Wednesday 15 July 2026
intro: Good morning and welcome to the Barefoot Daily News Bulletin for Wednesday the fifteenth of July, twenty twenty-six. Here is the good news from the water, the outdoors and the Top End.

stories.headline: Forty-nine tonnes of debris cleared from Torres Strait waters
stories.summary: The Australian Fisheries Management Authority and Parks Australia removed three abandoned foreign fishing vessels and associated gear from Crab Island, Horn Island and Pearce Cay during operations in March and April 2026, clearing about 49 tonnes of harmful marine debris. Crab Island is a vital nesting site for the flatback turtle, where a beached vessel was dismantled and steel material removed. An 80-metre net was recovered from the wreck at Pearce Cay and taken to a land-based facility for disposal.
stories.spoken: A major clean-up has cleared roughly forty-nine tonnes of harmful marine debris from waters in the Torres Strait. Working across autumn, the Australian Fisheries Management Authority and Parks Australia recovered three abandoned foreign fishing vessels and their gear from Crab Island, Horn Island and Pearce Cay. At Crab Island, an important flatback turtle nesting site, a beached vessel was dismantled and its steel removed, while an eighty-metre net was hauled from the wreck at Pearce Cay and taken ashore for disposal.

stories.headline: New artificial reef installed off Coffs Harbour
stories.summary: A new artificial reef has been installed off the Coffs Harbour coast to support tourism and sustainable recreational fishing along coastal New South Wales. It consists of two 50-tonne steel structures, each with a 15.6 by 15.6 metre footprint and a height of 12 metres, placed about 500 metres apart in 28 metres of water and engineered to withstand a one-in-100-year storm. The reef is designed to attract species including mackerel, snapper and mulloway, and joins twelve others already installed along the New South Wales coast.
stories.spoken: A new artificial reef has been placed off Coffs Harbour to boost coastal tourism and sustainable recreational fishing. It is made up of two steel structures, each weighing fifty tonnes and standing twelve metres tall, set about five hundred metres apart in twenty-eight metres of water and built to survive a one-in-one-hundred-year storm. Designed to draw in mackerel, snapper and mulloway, it becomes the thirteenth such reef along the New South Wales coast.

stories.headline: More than 200 'cod hotels' to rebuild fish habitat in South East Queensland
stories.summary: More than 200 purpose-built 'cod hotels' are being installed across South East Queensland waterways to restore habitat for native fish, including the critically endangered Mary River cod. The structures are made from recycled hollow logs and natural timber to mimic the sheltered environments fish use for breeding, feeding and protection, with the first now going into the Mid and Upper Brisbane River in the Somerset region. The work is delivered through the Resilient Rivers SEQ program, alongside additional projects such as a new 1.1 million dollar fishway at Gould Adams Park.
stories.spoken: More than two hundred purpose-built shelters known as cod hotels are being placed in South East Queensland waterways to rebuild habitat for native fish, among them the critically endangered Mary River cod. Made from recycled hollow logs and natural timber, they copy the sheltered spots fish rely on to breed, feed and hide, with the first now going into the Mid and Upper Brisbane River in the Somerset region. The effort is part of the Resilient Rivers program and sits alongside other work, including a new fishway at Gould Adams Park.
stories.source: The National Tribune (Council of Mayors SEQ)

stories.headline: Wellington Point angler shares a lifelong love of Moreton Bay
stories.summary: Wellington Point fisher AJ Amos-Maclean has spoken about her lifelong passion for fishing Moreton Bay and her interest in marine conservation. A recent outing produced a good-sized snapper, one of the species the bay is known for. Her story reflects the pleasure that recreational anglers take in long days on the water.
stories.spoken: A Wellington Point angler, AJ Amos-Maclean, has shared a lifelong love of fishing Moreton Bay, together with a keen interest in marine conservation. Among her recent rewards was a good-sized snapper, one of the species the bay is well known for. It is a reminder of the simple pleasure that keeps recreational anglers coming back to the water.

stories.headline: A quiet reflection on protecting the wild waters we fish
stories.summary: Writing in Hatch Magazine, Johnny Carrol Sain recalls a secluded creek that could once be found only by map, compass or trusted direction, and reflects on how easily crowds can erode the wild character of hidden fishing waters. The essay makes the case that unspoiled places carry a value beyond any profit, and that their magic is fragile. It is a meditation, from an angler's perspective, on why quiet waters deserve protection.
stories.spoken: In a thoughtful piece for Hatch Magazine, writer Johnny Carrol Sain remembers a hidden creek that could once be reached only by map, compass, or a trusted word, and considers how quickly crowds can wear away the wild feel of such places. His argument is a simple one: unspoiled water holds a worth beyond profit, and that magic is easily lost. It is a gentle reminder, from one angler to another, of why quiet waters are worth protecting.

stories.headline: Gone Fishing Weekend packages open for clubs in October
stories.summary: Fishing World reports that Gone Fishing Weekend packages are available to help fishing clubs and organisations run their own events to promote and celebrate fishing. The weekend falls on Saturday the seventeenth or Sunday the eighteenth of October 2026. The packages are aimed at encouraging community participation in recreational fishing.
stories.spoken: Something to look forward to for the clubs: packages are now available to help fishing clubs and organisations host their own events for Gone Fishing Weekend. The weekend lands on Saturday the seventeenth and Sunday the eighteenth of October, and the idea is simply to get more people out enjoying recreational fishing together.
